summary: The suite covers the main functional axes (basic cartesian product, exclude rules, include rules, full config with fail-fast/max-parallel/feature-flags, and three error paths) plus workflow-structure checks, giving reasonable breadth. However, several requirements go untested: invalid JSON input, an empty-after-exclusion matrix, a config with no cartesian axes (include-only), single-axis configs, exclude rules that match nothing, and default max_combinations=256 boundary behavior. Rigor is weak: most assertions are coarse string-presence checks rather than exact JSON parsing, and the boundary/edge-case set is thin. Design has serious problems: (1) the entire suite depends on one act invocation — any infrastructure failure makes every test fail; (2) block extraction via `grep -A N ... | grep -B N ...` with hardcoded line counts is notoriously unreliable and will silently miss content if the block exceeds N lines; (3) counting raw occurrences of `"os"` to infer the number of matrix entries is fragile — any surrounding output containing that string skews the count, and include entries that lack an 'os' key would undercount; (4) `act ... || true` silently swallows failures, so a broken act run still looks like a pass until a specific assertion fires. The workflow-structure tests (YAML grep, actionlint, file-existence) are well-targeted, but the act-output parsing approach undermines confidence that the suite would reliably catch regressions.
